summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – CONTRAVENTION – Where the applicant alleges that the respondent failed to return the parties children to the Commonwealth of Australia after being ordered to do so – Where the respondent concedes that she had contravened a Court order – Where the respondent contends that her contravention was vitiated by reasonable excuse – Where the Court finds that no reasonable excuse is established on the evidence – Where the Court makes orders for communication between the applicant and the children – Where the Court makes orders for further submissions about what orders or penalty are to be imposed.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

INTRODUCTION

These reasons for judgment explain why the Court has found the respondent to have contravened Orders made by Schonell J on 30 January 2025 (“the 30 January 2025 Orders”) beyond reasonable doubt and without reasonable excuse, and why the Court has made enforceable orders for telephone or video communication between the applicant and the children three nights per week.

BRIEF BACKGROUND

The parties are parents to two children, X born in Australia in 2017, and X born in 2020 (collectively “the children”). The children have resided with the respondent in Country B since July 2024, when she unilaterally retained them there. The applicant has remained in the Commonwealth of Australia.
The present proceedings arise from orders made by Schonell J on 30 January 2025 in determining competing interlocutory applications for parenting and financial orders (Rothman & Rothman [2025] FedCFamC1F 40 (“Rothman”)).
In Rothman, Schonell J determined that this Court had jurisdiction over the children by virtue of the provisions of s 69E of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), and that “the children were at all material times, up until their wrongful retention in Country B, habitually resident in Australia” (Rothman at [31]). It is only the parenting component of those orders that are relevant and bare reproduction:
The mother forthwith and within 7 days do all acts and things and sign all documents as shall be necessary to return [X] born [date redacted] 2017 and [Y] born [date redacted] 2020 (“the children”) to the Commonwealth of Australia and disembark at Sydney International Airport.
The mother do all acts and things to inform the Respondent father (“the father”) in writing of the children’s return flight details to Australia.
If the mother fails or neglects to comply with Order 1 above, the father shall be granted sole parental responsibility and primary care of the children in [Country B] for the purpose of returning the children to Australia.
The father be at liberty to make a copy of these orders available to such persons, agencies and organisations, in Australia, [Country B] or such other jurisdiction as the children are or may be taken, as is reasonably required to assist in the implementation of these orders, including but not limited to any airline on which the children travel or are booked or seek to travel.
Until further order the mother and the father, their servants and/or agents be and are hereby restrained by injunction, and irrespective of authenticated consent as contemplated in Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975, from removing or attempting to remove or causing or permitting the removal of the children from the Commonwealth of Australia,
AND IT IS REQUESTED THAT the Australian Federal Police give effect to this order by placing the names of the children on the Family Law Watchlist in force at all points of arrival and departure in the Commonwealth of Australia and maintain the children’s names on the Watchlist until the Court orders its removal.

Upon the mother returning the children to Australia
The mother and children shall have exclusive occupation of the property at [C Street], [Suburb D].

8. Until further order, the children live with the mother.
Until further order, the children shall spend time with the father each alternate weekend from after school or 3.00 pm on Friday until 5.00 pm on Sunday.

Liberty to Relist
The parties have liberty to relist this matter before me on 24 hours’ notice in relation to parenting orders.
The mother have liberty to relist this matter before me on 24 hours’ notice of her return to Australia to address any issues that she has or may arise in relation to the question of safety including, if necessary, the granting of injunctions.
There was no appellate challenge to the 30 January 2025 Orders. His Honour’s reasons of the same date fulsomely explain why those orders were made. It is common ground that the mother has not complied with the 30 January 2025 Orders, and that the children remain with the respondent in Country B to date.

CONTRAVENTION APPLICATION

The applicant filed an Application – Contravention on 11 March 2025 (“the contravention application”). The contravention application was listed before this Court by order of Campton J dated 29 April 2025.
I first heard this matter on 2 May 2025. Unfortunately, the parties were only informed about the listing of the contravention application on 29 April 2025, some four days before it was to be heard.
On 2 May 2025, the Court was unable to establish any communication with the respondent and was informed by the respondent’s counsel that she had not had an opportunity to communicate with her client directly prior to the hearing. In those circumstances, I adjourned the hearing to 5 September 2025 (see Rothman & Rothman (No 3) [2025] FedCFamC1F 297).
The matter came before this Court on 5 September 2025. Notwithstanding the frustration of this Court, the matter was again adjourned. On this occasion the adjournment was precipitated by the late filing of material by the respondent that would have materially prejudiced the applicant if he was not given an opportunity to adequately consider and respond. I made an order for costs thrown away, in the sum of $8,000, against the respondent and listed the contravention application for hearing on 6 February 2026 (see Rothman & Rothman (No 4) [2025] FedCFamC1F 636).
These proceedings were finally heard, without adjournment or delay, on 6 February 2026. Both parties were represented by counsel, with the applicant present in person and the respondent via audio-visual link.

ISSUE BEFORE THE COURT

At the outset of the 6 February 2026 hearing the respondent’s counsel conceded the existence of a prima facie case but contended for a reasonable excuse. In other words, the respondent admitted to contravening the 30 January 2025 Orders, but asserted a complete defence to the contravention in the form of a reasonable excuse under s 70NAD of the Act.
In such circumstances, the issue before the Court was significantly truncated, limited to only the question of whether the evidence adduced by the respondent satisfies the Court of a reasonable basis for her contravention of the 30 January 2025 Orders. The respondent bears the burden of proof in respect of any reasonable excuse defence (s 70NADA of the Act).

EVIDENCE

Written evidence

In furtherance of his case the applicant relied upon the following material:
(a) The contravention application filed 11 March 2025;

(b) Amended Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 22 October 2025;

(c) His affidavit filed 11 March 2025;

(d) His affidavit filed 24 September 2025;

(e) Proposed minute of order to be made in the event that the Court finds the respondent has contravened a Court order without reasonable excuse provided to Chambers on 10 February 2026 at 3:09 pm and marked as Exhibit C1; and

(f) Documents tendered from the applicant’s tender bundle provided to the Court on 1 May 2025 labelled as Exhibit A1.

Notwithstanding the operation of r 11.67 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth), given the quasi-criminal nature of contravention proceedings, nothing obliged the respondent to file a response. Accepting this, the respondent nonetheless chose to rely upon the following material:
(a) Amended Application in a Proceeding filed 24 September 2025;

(b) Her affidavit filed 4 September 2025; and

(c) Her affidavit filed 4 February 2026 (“the respondent’s updating affidavit”).

The Court notes that whilst the respondent was granted leave to rely on her late filed updating affidavit, paragraphs three, four, five and six of the same were struck out. The material contained therein existed at the time the respondent filed her 4 September 2025 affidavit and therefore could not be described as “updating”. The applicant’s counsel argued that the inclusion of the non-updating material was unfairly prejudicial given its late production. The Court agreed.

Cross-examination

The mother was cross-examined. Her attendance was delivered via audio-visual link, and she answered questions under oath. She impressed as an intelligent and articulate woman. Her evidence deposes to a high level of education. Notwithstanding this, she was a feisty witness and pervasively unresponsive.
A strong impression was formed by the Court that the respondent has opportunistically manipulated the legal system of Country B to her own ends by obtaining unilateral orders and by failing to disclose important evidence to those Courts such as the fact that the children were born in and habitual residents of the Commonwealth of Australia.

APPLICABLE LAW

The contravention application invokes Part VII Division 13A of the Act. The focus of a Court in dealing with such an application is on making orders that will enforce future compliance rather than punishing past behaviour (Winch & Jackson [2015] FamCAFC 75; (2015) FLC 93-649). Notwithstanding this objective (see s 70NAB of the Act), the possibility of sanction, imprisonment or other civil penalty consequent to any positive contravention finding renders these proceedings, at least analogous to, quasi-criminal proceedings (Saldo & Tindall [2013] FamCA 951 at [28]).

Reasonable excuse

The respondent concedes that she had contravened the 30 January 2025 Orders. It is thus not essential to reproduce s 70NAC of the Act. The respondent does however claim that she had, and has, a reasonable excuse for her contravening conduct.
Section 70NAD of the Act relevantly states:
70NAD Meaning of reasonable excuse for contravening a child‑related order
Where person did not understand obligations
(1) A person has a reasonable excuse for contravening a child‑related order if:
(a) the person contravened the order because at the time of the contravention the person did not understand the obligations imposed by the order; and

(b) the court considers that the person ought to be excused in respect of the contravention.

(2) If the court decides that a person has a reasonable excuse under subsection (1) for contravening a child‑related order, the court must explain to the person, in language likely to be readily understood by the person:
(a) the obligations imposed on the person by the order; and
(b) the consequences that may follow if the person contravenes the order again.

Protection of health or safety of a person
(3) A person has a reasonable excuse for contravening a child‑related order if:
(a) the person contravened the order because the person reasonably believed that the person’s actions constituting the contravention were necessary to protect the health or safety of the person, a child or any other person; and

(b) the period of the contravention was not longer than necessary to protect the health or safety of the person, child or other person.

Section does not limit circumstances of a reasonable excuse
(4) This section does not limit the circumstances in which a person may have a reasonable excuse for contravening a child‑related order.
When considering whether a reasonable excuse is established it is important to distinguish circumstances where a parent believes that time as ordered is not in a child’s best interests from situations where contravention of the orders is necessary to protect the health or safety of a child. If this distinction is not made, any parent who disagreed with the orders of the Court would have a reasonable excuse for not complying with those orders. A distinctly similar rationale applies in circumstances where a respondent parent claims that they do not, or did not, understand the obligations placed on them by orders of this Court.
The establishment of reasonable excuse involves the application of an objective standard. The mere belief a party may have that orders are not in a child’s best interests is not a reasonable excuse (Gaunt & Gaunt [1978] FamCA 97; (1978) FLC 90-468). In Savage & Savage [2017] FamCA 93 Foster J stated at [51]:
It is readily apparent that the Court must determine “reasonable excuse” objectively and disregard the alleged contravener’s own motives or rationale. There must be a determination of reasonable excuse based on belief on reasonable grounds.

DISCUSSION

Consequence of the conceded case

The respondent’s counsel no doubt on instructions and after giving advice on consequences, conceded the existence of a prima facie case at the outset of the hearing. One such consequence is the possibility, if this Court rejected her reasonable excuse defence, of a finding against the respondent beyond a reasonable doubt (s 70NBF(1)(d)). If the respondent had a contrary view, it was incumbent on her to explain to the Court how the evidence led could only result in a finding on the balance of probabilities (s 70NBF(1)). No such submission was made, and the Court cannot see how the evidence would have supported it anyway.

The respondent’s evidence

On an objective view, the mother’s contended reasonable excuse case must have been one under s 70NAD(3) or s 70NAD(4) of the Act. On either hand, it is the view of this Court that the mother’s defence must fail. Simply, her evidence did not adequately dispel the burden of proof laid upon her by s 70NADA of the Act.
No evidence was led which explained why the protection of the health and safety of the children, or of herself, could only be achieved in Country B, but not in the Commonwealth of Australia. This was a point raised by Schonell J in Rothman at [52]:
... I note that the mother raises serious issues about matters of family violence. I am unable to resolve the factual contest, but I am satisfied that any order I make would promote the safety of the children and the mother in circumstances where there exists in this country the capacity to seek an ADVO on behalf of the mother and/or the children should that be necessary. In that respect, I note the mother’s evidence that she has on two prior occasions sought ADVO’s. To the extent that it is necessary, I will grant the mother liberty to restore the matter to the list on 24 hours’ notice of her return to Australia to address any issues that she has or may arise in relation to the question of safety including, if necessary, the granting of injunctions. Should the mother wish to return to [Country B] upon her return of the children to Australia, she is perfectly entitled to do so.
This very proposition was put to the respondent in cross-examination. She disagreed with the notion that Apprehended Domestic Violence Orders (“ADVO”) were sufficient protection for her, and the children’s, health and wellbeing (Transcript 6 February 2026, p. 17 lines 1–13).
This Court disagrees with the respondent’s contention, and notes that it made such disagreement abundantly clear to her counsel during the hearing:
HIS HONOUR: The contention she makes, based on your opening, is that under 70NAD, subsection (3), she contravened the order because the person reasonably believed that the person’s actions constituting the contravention were necessary to protect the health or safety of a person, a child or any other person.

So presumably, it is her safety and the safety of the children. Right. But that involves my accepting that the only way that she could protect herself was to go back to [Country B] and that there were no remedies available to her in Australia. Now, that’s a big assertion to make, especially when we know that she has used the system before – I mean, legitimately used the system. I don’t read a complaint about the inadequacy of the system, but again, please, if I’ve missed it in your client’s affidavit, I would like you to point it out.

So, you know, if I allow – if I say this is a reasonable excuse, I’m saying that it is a reasonable excuse for anybody to have subjective concerns about the adequacy of our system to protect the victims of violence and giving them licence to do whatever they want, including going to the other end of the world. Now, I would have to be really, really convinced on the evidence that to do that – I’m not saying it can’t be done. What I’m saying is I’m struggling to see how, on the evidence that I’ve read in your client’s affidavits and the evidence that I heard this morning, that it amounts to a reasonable excuse.

(Transcript 6 February 2026, p. 43 lines 9–29)

...

HIS HONOUR: But let’s assume I agree with you 100 percent. All right. Let’s assume – and it’s not a big assumption to make – that if I found these facts, you know, I would consider the father’s conduct wholly unacceptable. But the question I have to decide is whether it was reasonable that her actions were necessary. And in circumstances where – again, help me if I’m wrong – she expresses no dissatisfaction about the protections that were afforded to her with the previous ADVOs, where she says, “You know, I can’t get this, I can’t get that, in terms of protection here in Australia” – where she says nothing about that in her affidavit – correct me if I’m wrong - - -

(Transcript 6 February 2026, p. 44 lines 27–36).
It must be remembered, the issue for present purposes is not whether the respondent and the children experienced family violence perpetrated by the applicant. Such a finding was not possible in the hearing before Schonell J, nor is it in this hearing before me. I am solely tasked with forming a view about whether the respondent discharges the burden of proof based on objective considerations of the circumstances surrounding the respondent’s admitted contravention of Court orders.
The Court takes the mother’s case about family violence at its highest, as it must. Justice Schonell did the same in the 30 January 2025 Orders, wherein at Order 11 his Honour expressly contemplated relisting the matter on 24–hours’ notice to, in part, make injunctions for the respondent’s and the children’s personal safety. These orders were not disturbed by this Court, nor the subject of argument between the parties.
It is difficult to see how any reasonable person would discern an objective basis for the mother’s conduct on her evidence. Indeed, after the conclusion of the cross-examination of the respondent the Court directly asked the respondent’s counsel “your case doesn’t get any better than it is at this point, does it? I’m talking about the contravention application. It’s at its highest point” (Transcript 6 February 2026, p. 40 lines 31–33). The Court received an equivocal, but undeniably affirmative response to this inquiry.

Deficiencies in the respondent’s case

The respondent’s case fails to engage with three key issues. Firstly, if the reasonable excuse is to be categorised under s 70NAD(3) of the Act, why the unilateral retention of the children in Country B was the only reasonable way for her to achieve adequate protection. Secondly, for any s 70NAD(3) reasonable excuse, why her unilateral retention of the children since July 2024 was “not longer than necessary to protect the health or safety” of the children or herself (s 70NAD(3)(b) of the Act). Thirdly, for any broad s 70NAD(4) reasonable excuse defence, why the Court would exercise its discretion in the respondent’s favour on the evidence before it.
The respondent’s own evidence establishes that she has engaged with the Australian legal system previously, and indeed successfully obtained two ADVOs against the applicant. On both occasions the respondent had contact with local police services. The respondent makes no complaint about the adequacy of those protective services anywhere in her evidence. Whilst she may well feel subjectively safe in Country B, the test for present purposes is objective. It must be so, otherwise litigants could take the law into their own hands and simply leave the jurisdiction without accountability.
Even assuming the Court has found wrongly as to the merits of the respondent’s s 70NAD(3)(a) claim, the respondent would fail to satisfy the second limb under s 70NAD(3)(b) of the Act. There is nothing stopping the respondent from returning to the Commonwealth of Australia forthwith. The 30 January 2025 Orders objectively establish safety for the respondent and the children. Should they return to the Commonwealth of Australia, the respondent and the children will have exclusive occupation of their former family home. Further, the children are ordered to live with the respondent, and the Court has granted the respondent the indulgence of leave to relist on 24–hour’s notice in this Court which has its own protective powers.
It is apparent that this Court has jurisdiction over the children and has offered its protective powers to the respondent and the children should they demonstrate that it is necessary. Above and beyond this, the state of New South Wales has already demonstrated a willingness to exercise powers in its ambit to protect the respondent and the children as evidenced by the ADVOs previously made out against the applicant. There is no objective basis for the respondent to believe that her safety currently at risk in the Commonwealth of Australia. Plainly, the period that the respondent has been in contravention of the 30 January 2025 Orders is longer than necessary.

Scope of the Court’s consideration

Doing the best the Court can to discern any substance in the mother’s argument, there was a veiled contention that s 70NAD(4) of the Act would allow the Court to consider the best interests of the children generally in concluding that there was a reasonable excuse. The Court can only assume that the respondent’s counsel misread the direct text of s 70NAD(4).
The provision states that, “This section does not limit the circumstances in which a person may have a reasonable excuse for contravening a child‑related order”. It does not say that contravention orders are parenting orders; s 64B of the Act, in the sense that s 60CA of the Act becomes a mandatory consideration. Section 70NAB outlines a separate objects clause which informs the application of the Court’s contravention powers. These objects, whilst similar in tenor to those elsewhere in the Act, are not the same as those governing the Act’s parenting powers. Moreover, the objects clearly emphasise that best interests are met by supporting compliance with Court orders (s 70NAB(a) of the Act) and deterring non-compliance with Court orders (s 70NAB(c) of the Act).
Further, s 70NAD(4) refers to not limiting the circumstances a person may have for any contravention. It does not open the floodgates for the Court to consider any idiosyncratic factors it may like in determining whether a reasonable excuse is made out on an objective basis. It is not correct to suggest that the word circumstances in s 70NAD(4) ought to be read as lowering the task of the Court from a consideration of objective circumstances to one of varied subjective impression.
The Court rejects any attempt by the respondent to use the contravention application as a “trial run” for any application for international relocation she should or could have pursued rather than the unilateral relocation that she did.

Finding

The respondent’s reasonable excuse defence is not established. The Court makes this finding on the higher “beyond a reasonable doubt” standard. No reasonable person would conclude that there was adequate objective basis for the mother’s initial or continuing contravention of the 30 January 2026 Orders.
Should the Court be incorrect on this point, it would have found the respondent had contravened orders of this Court without reasonable excuse on the balance of probabilities. The practical distinction between in the Court making clear the standard upon which its findings are made is the viability of greater civil penalty and/or imprisonment under s 70NBF(4) of the Act.

ORDERS

Heads of power

Section 70NBA of the Act provides certain orders that a Court can make in contravention proceedings regardless of any finding of fault:
70NBA Court may make orders in proceedings relating to contravention of child‑related orders
(1) This Subdivision sets out orders that a court exercising jurisdiction in proceedings under this Act may make if:
(a) an issue arises in the proceedings about whether a person (the respondent) has contravened a child‑related order; and

(b) a party to the proceedings makes an application for an order under this Subdivision in relation to the issue.

Note: The court does not need to find that the respondent contravened a child‑related order to make certain orders under this Subdivision. For example, the court may make the following orders at any stage of the proceedings:

(a) a make‑up time parenting order under section 70NBB;

(b) an order varying or suspending a parenting order under section 70NBC;
(c) an order requiring the respondent and any other party to the proceedings to attend a post‑separation parenting program under section 70NBD.
(2) Each of the following is a child‑related order:
(a) a parenting order;

(b) an injunction granted by the court:
(i) under section 68B; or
(ii) under section 114 in so far as the injunction is for the protection of a child;
(c) a bond entered into:
(i) under a parenting order; or

(ii) under paragraph 70NBF(1)(a); or

(iii) for the purposes of subsection 70NCC(3);
(d) an undertaking given to, and accepted by, the court that relates to, or to the making of, an order, injunction or bond referred to in any of paragraphs (a) to (c);
(e) a subpoena issued under the applicable Rules of Court that:
(i) relates to, or to the making of, an order, injunction or bond referred to in any of paragraphs (a) to (c); and

(ii) is issued to a party to the proceedings for the order, injunction or bond, as the case may be

Having found that the respondent contravened the 30 January 2026 Orders beyond a reasonable doubt it is necessary to consider s 70NBF of the Act:
70NBF Orders where contravention established without reasonable excuse

Orders for contraventions without reasonable excuse
(1) If the court finds that the respondent has contravened a child‑related order without having a reasonable excuse, the court may make any of the following orders:
(a) an order requiring the respondent to enter into a bond in accordance with section 70NCA;

(b) if an order is made under paragraph (a), and the respondent fails, without having a reasonable excuse, to enter into the bond—an order imposing a fine not exceeding 10 penalty units on the respondent;

(c) where the contravention resulted in a child not spending time with, or living with, a person (the affected person) for a period—an order requiring the respondent to compensate the affected person for some or all of any expenses the affected person reasonably incurred as a result of the contravention;

(d) where the court is satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the respondent contravened the order:

(i) an order imposing a fine not exceeding 60 penalty units;

(ii) an order imposing a term of imprisonment.
Note: For subparagraph (d)(ii), see subsection (4), subsection 70NCC(2) and section 70NCE for limits on how and when the court may impose a term of imprisonment on the respondent.
Matters to be considered by the court

(2) In making an order mentioned in subsection (1), the court must have regard to:
(a) the likely effects of making the order on any child, or any other person; and
(b) the seriousness of the contravention.
(3) Without limiting the matters the court may take into account, the following matters must be taken into account by the court when having regard to the seriousness of the contravention:
(a) whether a court has previously found that the respondent has contravened a child‑related order without having a reasonable excuse;

(b) whether the respondent behaved in a way that showed a serious disregard of the respondent’s obligations under the child‑related order mentioned in subsection (1);

(c) the behaviour of any person with whom the child is to live or spend time under the child‑related order mentioned in subsection (1).

(4) The court may sentence the respondent to imprisonment under subparagraph (1)(d)(ii) only if the court is satisfied that, in all the circumstances of the case, it would not be appropriate for the court to deal with the contravention in any other way under subsection (1).

This Court is aware of no authority that suggests that, despite finding that the respondent has contravened orders of this Court without reasonable excuse beyond a reasonable doubt, that the penalties open in the event of the same finding on the balance of probabilities would not also be within the remit of the Court to impose.

Submissions

Counsel for the respondent contended that no order should be made. Whilst the Court accepts that the inclusion of the word “may” in s 70NBF(1) the Act endows the Court with wide discretion as to the penalty to impose; including the possibility of no penalty whatsoever, it was incumbent on the respondent’s counsel to make fulsome submissions as to why the Court should exercise its powers in this manner. The specifics of the respondent’s contention were left for the Court to infer as opposed to articulated.
The applicant contended for the following order:
In respect of the contravention of order 2 of the orders made on 30 January 2025 that subject to the Respondent receiving an explanation in accordance with s. 70NCA (5) of the Family Law Act 1975 (C’th) (“the Act”) the Respondent is required to enter into a bond with/without surety/security in accordance with s. 70NCA of the Act upon the following condition:
(a) To be for a period of two (2) years/other lesser period if so determined

(b) To be of good behaviour
(c) To forfeit the sum of $ (figure to be inserted) to the Commonwealth of Australia should the Respondent fail to comply with any term of the bond
(Exhibit C1).
Counsel for the applicant submitted that the proposed orders were uncertain in effect and arguably vague.
It is clear to the Court that both parties need to consider the issue of Orders or penalty more fulsomely, especially after contemplating these reasons of judgment. This can be accommodated through written submissions with the opportunity for short oral submissions if the Court so requires.
The initial written submissions should be limited to 1000 words by each party. The applicant shall be provided fourteen days to file and serve their written submissions. Fourteen days thereafter, the respondent shall file and serve their submissions of equal length. In the interest of procedural fairness, the applicant may have a final brief reply not exceeding 200 words to be filed and served within seven days thereafter.

VARIATION AND SUSPENSION OF PARENTING ORDERS

Section 70NBC of the Act allows this Court to vary or suspend parenting orders between the parties. It states as follows:
70NBC Variation and suspension of child‑related orders that are parenting orders

Variation of child‑related orders that are parenting orders
(1) The court may, at any stage of the proceedings, vary a child‑related order that is a parenting order.
Note: The court must not reconsider a final parenting order (other than a child maintenance order) unless the court has considered certain matters and is satisfied of certain matters: see section 65DAAA.
(2) Subsection (1) does not limit the circumstances in which a court having jurisdiction under this Act may vary a child‑related order that is a parenting order.
Suspension of child‑related orders that are parenting orders
(3) The court may, at any stage of the proceedings, suspend for a specified period of time the operation of, or part of the operation of, a child‑related order that is a parenting order.
Note: The court must not reconsider a final parenting order (other than a child maintenance order) unless the court has considered certain matters and is satisfied of certain matters: see section 65DAAA
The applicant proposed, in the face of little constructive opposition from the respondent, that orders for communication between the applicant and the children via telephone or video call. The evidence of both parties illustrates that the current informal arrangement between them has been problematic for various reasons including issues of practicality, the ages of the children, and what the Court concludes from the evidence of the respondent in cross-examination is a distinct lack of enthusiasm for facilitating communication with the applicant.
The Court is satisfied that it is in the best interests of the children that specific enforceable orders be made which, hopefully, will facilitate communication between the children and the applicant for as long as the children remain in Country B, and the applicant in the Commonwealth of Australia.
During the hearing dialogue arose between the Court and respondent’s counsel which is relevant to the issue of dates and timings of any proposed communication orders:
But you’re saying I shouldn’t make any other order. Now, if I were thinking about making – using the powers I’ve got to vary the parenting orders, I would probably be trying to fix up this video communication between the father and the children. It doesn’t seem to be working really well, does it?

MS COULTON: No. Well, both of them say that there has been video contact.

HIS HONOUR: Yes. Difficulties.

MS COULTON: And it has been agreed on the Thursday night, Friday night and Sunday, and that has been happening.

HIS HONOUR: Yes. So I could amend the orders to reflect that, couldn’t I, based on the evidence that I’ve heard.

MS COULTON: Yes.

HIS HONOUR: Yes. Okay. I think, perhaps, based on the evidence I might be thinking that your client’s commitment to this needs to be dialled up a little bit, so I might use a little bit stronger language than what is proposed in her order, but at least that facilitates the communication between the father and the children, doesn’t it?

MS COULTON: Yes, your Honour.

(Transcript 13 February 2026, p. 47 lines 12–34)
Indeed, very similar dialogue arose between the Court and the applicant’s counsel:
And so in my respectful submission, what you would do is fix up the communication order, such that there is a time for these calls to occur between 7.30 and 8.00 pm Sydney time, three days per week as agreed, but failing agreement, on Thursday, Friday and Monday.

HIS HONOUR: Sorry. Thursday, Friday, Sunday, I think it was.

MR BLACKAH: Sunday. I apologise. Thursday, Friday, Sunday.

HIS HONOUR: And all at the same time?

MR BLACKAH: Yes, because Thursday, Friday, he’s at work.

HIS HONOUR: Yes.

MR BLACKAH: He can’t do earlier. And for consistency .... you would also restrain the mother from being present, except in order to establish the call for the children, and then to leave and give them privacy.

(Transcript 6 February 2026, p.49 lines 28–45).
In light of this exchange, and the evidence before the Court, I find that the applicant should be permitted to have telephone or video communication with the children as agreed between the parties in writing, but in default, between 7:30 pm and 8:00 pm local Sydney time on Thursday, Friday and Sunday nights weekly whilst he is not otherwise spending time with the children.
The applicant is to initiate the telephone or video communication and shall be permitted to speak to the children without the respondent overhearing or interfering in the call. If the children do not have a device capable of facilitating telephone or video communication, the respondent is to provide the children with any device she owns capable of facilitating such calls for the entire duration of the scheduled call.

CONCLUSION

The Court will consider any consequential orders, or impose any warranted penalties, once further submissions are received.
